title: Analytical Model of Clock Drift in Quantum Key Distribution and a Simple Synchronization Algorithm
authors: Loïc Millet, Boris Korzh, Rob Thew, Gianluca Boso
abstract: Clock synchronization is critical for maintaining low error rates in quantum key distribution. Here, we describe how a frequency mismatch between the transmitter and receiver clocks affects the quantum bit error rate in quantum key distribution, and derive from this model a simple synchronization algorithm together with clock stability requirements for practical operation. Our algorithm continuously compensates for both frequency mismatch and time-offset fluctuations directly from detection timestamps. It does not require a dedicated synchronization channel or auxiliary qubit sequence, converges from a large frequency mismatch within approximately one second of photon acquisition, and remains effective in low-photon-count regimes (more than 30 dB of channel loss) using standard hardware. We validate our approach by demonstrating successful key exchange over 100 km of fiber and continuous operation over 24 hours in a 16 km metropolitan network using commercial systems, with performance equivalent to using a service channel for clock synchronization.
summary: This paper presents an analytical model and a practical algorithm to synchronize clocks in quantum key distribution without needing extra communication channels. By using detection timestamps to compensate for frequency mismatch, the method enables robust, long-term QKD operation even in high-loss fiber networks.
main_problem: Clock frequency mismatch between transmitter and receiver in time-bin QKD causes time shifts that increase the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER). The goal is to develop a synchronization method that does not require a dedicated service channel or auxiliary qubit sequence.
main_result: The authors developed a lightweight synchronization algorithm that recovers clock drift and compensates for time offsets directly from detection timestamps, achieving stable operation over 24 hours in a metropolitan network with performance equivalent to a dedicated service channel.
method: The paper uses an analytical model of clock drift and a synchronization algorithm based on the circular means of consecutive folded histograms to estimate frequency mismatch and time-offset fluctuations.
model_or_system: A fiber-based time-bin BB84 QKD system using commercial hardware (ID Quantique Clavis XG) including gain-switched DFB lasers, modulators, and SPADs, deployed in both laboratory (100 km fiber) and field (16 km Geneva Quantum Network) settings.
key_observables: Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), photon detection-time probability distribution, clock drift rate, and Time Deviation (TDEV).
important_parameters: Frequency mismatch (delta f), time-bin duration (T_bin), integration time (T_int), channel loss (up to 30 dB), and detector jitter.
main_assumptions: The frequency mismatch is assumed constant over a single integration time, and the clock drift follows a linear/aging model where the shift between consecutive acquisitions is less than half a bin width.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 illustrates the PDF of photon arrival times under different drift levels; Figure 6 demonstrates the 24-hour stability of the GQN network (drift, detection time, and QBER); Figure 7 shows the error in drift estimation with and without Poissonian noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of clock drift, presents an analytical model for the resulting QBER, describes the proposed synchronization algorithm, details the experimental setup and field deployment, and concludes with error analysis and stability constraints.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Experimental setup of the QKD systems. GS DFB: gain-switched distributed feedback laser; IM: intensity modulator; PM: phase modulator; SPD: single-photon detector; FPGA: field-programmable gate array; TDC: time-to-digital converter used for time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) histogram acquisition. Dashed lines represent temperature stabilized regions.
FIG. 2: Probability density functions for ∆tdrift(Tint) = 0.5 ns (top) and 1 ns (bottom). Dashed lines indicate a filtering window of width w = 300 ps.

FIG. 3: (Top) ϵ(w) drift as a function of ∆tdrift(Tint) for different filtering window widths w at z = 120 km. (Bottom) ∆tdrift(ϵ(w) thr = 0.1 %) as a function of the transmission distance z for different filtering window widths w.
FIG. 5: Average detection time (top) and clock drift (bottom) as a function of the quantum channel loss. Shaded region indicates the standard deviation of the Bob-reference curve.
FIG. 4: Measured QBER with (w = 300 ps) and without temporal filtering as a function of quantum channel loss. Shaded region indicates the standard deviation of the Alice-reference curve.
